section.title: Blessings and Curses
section.sectionRationale: This chapter closes the Holiness Code with the covenant's blessings for obedience and escalating curses for disobedience, structured like an ancient treaty, ending remarkably with hope: even in exile, if Israel confesses and humbles their hearts, God will remember His covenant.
section.thesis: God sets before Israel a real choice, rich blessing for covenant obedience and escalating discipline for persistent rebellion, yet even His severest judgment aims at repentance, and He promises never to finally break His covenant, remembering Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob even when Israel is scattered among the nations.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the basic command against idolatry and for keeping the Sabbath; the blessings promised for obedience; the escalating curses for persistent disobedience, given in five waves; and the closing promise that confession and humbled hearts will move God to remember His ancient covenant.

units.label: No idols, and reverence for the sanctuary

verses.text: “You must not make idols for yourselves or set up a carved image or sacred pillar; you must not place a sculpted stone in your land to bow down to it. For I am the LORD your God.
You must keep My Sabbaths and have reverence for My sanctuary. I am the LORD.

units.label: Blessings for obedience
units.note: rain, harvest, peace, God dwelling among them

media.alt: A field heavy with grain under open sky
media.caption: God promises rain, harvest, peace, and His own dwelling presence for covenant faithfulness.

verses.text: If you follow My statutes and carefully keep My commandments,
I will give you rains in their season, and the land will yield its produce, and the trees of the field will bear their fruit.
Your threshing will continue until the grape harvest, and the grape harvest will continue until sowing time; you will have your fill of food to eat and will dwell securely in your land.
And I will give peace to the land, and you will lie down with nothing to fear. I will rid the land of dangerous animals, and no sword will pass through your land.
You will pursue your enemies, and they will fall by the sword before you.
Five of you will pursue a hundred, and a hundred of you will pursue ten thousand, and your enemies will fall by the sword before you.
I will turn toward you and make you fruitful and multiply you, and I will establish My {{berit|covenant}} with you.
You will still be eating the old supply of grain when you need to clear it out to make room for the new.
And I will make My dwelling place among you, and My soul will not despise you.
I will walk among you and be your God, and you will be My people.
I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt so that you would no longer be slaves to the Egyptians. I broke the bars of your yoke and enabled you to walk in uprightness.
If, however, you fail to obey Me and to carry out all these commandments,
and if you reject My statutes, despise My ordinances, and neglect to carry out all My commandments, and so break My covenant,

then this is what I will do to you: I will bring upon you sudden terror, wasting disease, and fever that will destroy your sight and drain your life. You will sow your seed in vain, because your enemies will eat it.
And I will set My face against you, so that you will be defeated by your enemies. Those who hate you will rule over you, and you will flee when no one pursues you.
And if after all this you will not obey Me, I will proceed to punish you sevenfold for your sins.
I will break down your stubborn pride and make your sky like iron and your land like bronze,
and your strength will be spent in vain. For your land will not yield its produce, and the trees of the land will not bear their fruit.
If you walk in {{qeri|hostility}} toward Me and refuse to obey Me, I will multiply your plagues seven times, according to your sins.
I will send wild animals against you to rob you of your children, destroy your livestock, and reduce your numbers, until your roads lie desolate.
And if in spite of these things you do not accept My discipline, but continue to walk in hostility toward Me,
then I will act with hostility toward you, and I will strike you sevenfold for your sins.

And I will bring a sword against you to execute the vengeance of the covenant. Though you withdraw into your cities, I will send a plague among you, and you will be delivered into the hand of the enemy.
When I cut off your supply of bread, ten women will bake your bread in a single oven and dole out your bread by weight, so that you will eat but not be satisfied.

units.label: The final curses, and scattering
units.note: the worst that unrepented sin brings

verses.text: But if in spite of all this you do not obey Me, but continue to walk in hostility toward Me,
then I will walk in fury against you, and I, even I, will punish you sevenfold for your sins.
You will eat the flesh of your own sons and daughters.
I will destroy your high places, cut down your incense altars, and heap your lifeless bodies on the lifeless remains of your idols; and My soul will despise you.
I will reduce your cities to rubble and lay waste your sanctuaries, and I will refuse to smell the pleasing aroma of your sacrifices.
And I will lay waste the land, so that your enemies who dwell in it will be appalled.
But I will scatter you among the nations and will draw out a sword after you as your land becomes desolate and your cities are laid waste.
Then the land shall enjoy its Sabbaths all the days it lies desolate, while you are in the land of your enemies. At that time the land will rest and enjoy its Sabbaths.
As long as it lies desolate, the land will have the rest it did not receive during the Sabbaths when you lived in it.

As for those of you who survive, I will send a faintness into their hearts in the lands of their enemies, so that even the sound of a windblown leaf will put them to flight. And they will flee as one flees the sword, and fall when no one pursues them.
They will stumble over one another as before the sword, though no one is behind them. So you will not be able to stand against your enemies.
You will perish among the nations, and the land of your enemies will consume you.
Those of you who survive in the lands of your enemies will waste away in their iniquity and will decay in the sins of their fathers.

units.label: Confession, and God remembers His covenant

media.alt: A distant land under a returning light
media.caption: Even scattered among the nations, God promises to remember His covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.

verses.text: But if they will confess their iniquity and that of their fathers in the unfaithfulness that they practiced against Me, by which they have also walked in hostility toward Me,
and I acted with hostility toward them and brought them into the land of their enemies, and if their {{arel|uncircumcised}} hearts will be humbled and they will make amends for their iniquity,
then I will {{zakar|remember}} My covenant with Jacob and My covenant with Isaac and My covenant with Abraham, and I will remember the land.

For the land will be abandoned by them, and it will enjoy its Sabbaths by lying desolate without them. And they will pay the penalty for their iniquity, because they rejected My ordinances and abhorred My statutes.
Yet in spite of this, when they are in the land of their enemies, I will not reject or despise them so as to destroy them and break My covenant with them; for I am the LORD their God.
But for their sake I will remember the covenant with their fathers, whom I brought out of the land of Egypt in the sight of the nations, that I might be their God. I am the LORD.”
These are the statutes, ordinances, and laws that the LORD established between Himself and the Israelites through Moses on Mount Sinai.

berit.gloss: Covenant, a binding relational agreement. This chapter functions as a covenant treaty's blessing and curse section, and its closing hope rests entirely on God's own commitment never to finally break the berit He made with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.

qeri.gloss: Hostility, or walking contrary. A rare word appearing seven times in this chapter alone, describing a settled, ongoing posture of opposition to God, not a single lapse, which is what provokes the escalating stages of discipline.

arel.gloss: Uncircumcised, used here metaphorically of the heart. Just as physical circumcision marked covenant belonging, an uncircumcised heart pictures a stubborn, unyielding resistance to God that must be humbled before restoration can begin.

zakar.gloss: To remember, not merely to recall but to act on behalf of. When God says He will remember His covenant, He means He will move to keep His ancient promise, grounding Israel's hope for restoration entirely in His own faithfulness, not their performance.

basic.context: This chapter closes the Holiness Code with a covenant treaty's typical structure: rich blessings promised for obedience, followed by five escalating waves of curses for persistent disobedience, ending in exile, and then, remarkably, a promise that confession and humbled hearts will move God to remember His covenant.
basic.hermeneutics: This describes Israel's specific old covenant terms tied to occupying the land of Canaan; it does not promise material prosperity for individually obedient Christians today, nor does it teach that every hardship signals God's specific curse, a caution Job and John 9:1-3 also raise. Its enduring principle, that sin brings real consequences and repentance brings real restoration, is fulfilled completely in Christ.
basic.originalLanguages: Berit, covenant, frames the whole chapter as a treaty's terms. Qeri, a rare word for settled hostility, appears seven times, marking what provokes escalating discipline. Arel, uncircumcised, describes the heart that must be humbled before restoration, and zakar, to remember, describes God acting to keep His ancient covenant despite Israel's unfaithfulness.
basic.typology: The curses for covenant unfaithfulness, culminating in exile, anticipate the ultimate curse Christ bore in His people's place. The uncircumcised heart that must be humbled anticipates the new covenant promise of a heart circumcised by the Spirit through Christ.
basic.christ: Galatians 3:13-14 says Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us, so that the blessing of Abraham might come to the nations. The curses this chapter describes in such detail were fully borne by Christ, so that the blessings this chapter promises are secured for His people forever.
basic.god: God is not capricious; He publishes in advance exactly what covenant faithfulness and covenant breaking will bring, so Israel has no excuse for surprise. His discipline escalates in deliberate stages, always aimed at bringing Israel back to Him, and even His harshest judgment is not His last word: He remains bound to remember His ancient promise.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 28 gives the fuller parallel of blessings and curses. Galatians 3:13-14 says Christ became a curse for us. Deuteronomy 30:1-6 promises future restoration and a circumcised heart. Romans 2:28-29 says true circumcision is of the heart, by the Spirit.
basic.oneStory: God set before Israel a real, serious choice with real consequences, yet built into the very terms of judgment a promise that His covenant faithfulness would outlast Israel's unfaithfulness. That promise finds its full and certain fulfillment in Christ, who bore the covenant's curse so that its blessing might reach everyone who trusts Him.

context.literary: This chapter follows the pattern of ancient Near Eastern treaty documents, which typically closed with blessings for loyalty and curses for rebellion, and it closes the entire Holiness Code, chapters 17 through 26, with the most sweeping statement yet of the stakes involved in covenant faithfulness.
context.historical: This chapter's escalating warnings, culminating in siege, exile, and scattering among the nations, would be realized in Israel's later history, first in the Assyrian exile of the northern kingdom and then in the Babylonian exile of Judah, exactly as this chapter and Deuteronomy 28 anticipated.
context.canonical: This chapter's structure and content are echoed and expanded in Deuteronomy 28 through 30, and its closing promise, that confession leads to God remembering His covenant, anchors the entire prophetic hope of return from exile and, ultimately, the new covenant.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To set before Israel, in the form of a covenant treaty's blessings and curses, the real and serious consequences of their coming choice to keep or break the covenant given at Sinai, while assuring them in advance that God's covenant faithfulness would outlast even their worst unfaithfulness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This chapter describes the specific old covenant terms of blessing and curse tied to Israel's occupation of the land of Canaan; it does not prescribe a formula guaranteeing material prosperity for obedient individuals today, nor does it prescribe reading every present hardship as a specific divine curse. Its enduring principle, that sin has real consequences and that repentance brings real restoration, continues to instruct, fulfilled completely in Christ, who bore the ultimate covenant curse.

debates.question: Should the specific material blessings and curses in this chapter be read as promises Christians should expect in their own material circumstances today?

views.label: Covenant specific to national Israel's land tenure
views.case: These blessings and curses are the specific terms of Israel's covenant with God tied to occupying the literal land of Canaan; they are not a general promise that obedient believers today will always prosper materially, nor that hardship always signals God's specific covenant curse, a caution Job and John 9:1-3 also raise.

views.label: A pattern illustrating that sin has real, corporate consequences
views.case: While the specific material terms were unique to Israel's land covenant, many see an abiding principle here: unrepentant, corporate rejection of God does bring real consequences, even if not according to this chapter's precise old covenant terms, and repentance does bring real restoration.

debates.bearing: Both views reject reading this chapter as a direct promise of material prosperity for individual obedient Christians today; they differ chiefly on how much general, non-specific principle about sin's consequences and repentance's restoration should be drawn from Israel's specific old covenant terms.

study.originalLanguages: Berit, covenant, frames this whole chapter as the blessing and curse section of a covenant treaty. Qeri, a rare Hebrew term for settled hostility, appears seven times, marking the specific provocation, ongoing defiance, not a single failure, that escalates God's discipline through each stage. Arel, uncircumcised, describes the heart that must be humbled before restoration, and zakar, to remember, describes God's decisive action to keep His ancient covenant despite Israel's unfaithfulness.

typology.type: The curses for covenant unfaithfulness, culminating in exile
typology.fulfillment: Christ became a curse for us, bearing the covenant curse His people deserved, so that the blessing of Abraham might come to all who believe, Galatians 3:13-14.

typology.type: The uncircumcised heart that must be humbled before restoration
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the new covenant promise of a circumcised heart, accomplished by the Spirit through Christ, Deuteronomy 30:6, Romans 2:29, Colossians 2:11.

typology.type: God's remembering His covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob despite Israel's unfaithfulness
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the new covenant's unbreakable foundation in Christ's own faithfulness rather than Israel's performance, Hebrews 8:6, 2 Timothy 2:13.

study.christ: Galatians 3:13-14 declares that Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law, having become a curse for us, so that in Him the blessing of Abraham might come to the nations. This chapter's detailed, escalating curses show exactly what covenant breaking deserves; Christ absorbed that entire curse Himself, so that everyone united to Him receives, instead, the blessing this chapter promises to the faithful.
study.god: God is not capricious or arbitrary; He publishes in advance, in painstaking detail, exactly what covenant faithfulness and covenant breaking will bring, so that Israel could never claim surprise. His discipline escalates deliberately, stage by stage, always aimed at provoking repentance rather than mere punishment, and even His harshest judgment, scattering His people among the nations, is not His final word: He remains bound by His own character to remember the covenant He swore to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.

crossReferences.note: Gives the fuller, parallel structure of blessings and curses for the same covenant.
Christ became a curse for us, redeeming us from the law's curse, so the blessing of Abraham might come to the nations.
Promises future restoration from exile and a heart circumcised by God, anticipating the new covenant.
True circumcision is a matter of the heart, by the Spirit, not merely the flesh.

study.oneStory: God set before Israel a real, serious choice with real and escalating consequences, yet built into the very terms of judgment a promise that His covenant faithfulness would outlast Israel's unfaithfulness. That promise finds its full and certain fulfillment in Christ, who bore the covenant's curse in full so that its blessing might reach everyone, from every nation, who turns to Him in repentant faith.

quiz.q: What two basic commands open this chapter, in verses 1-2?

options.text: Do not make idols, and keep My Sabbaths and reverence My sanctuary.
options.note: Verses 1-2 state these two commands directly.

options.text: Build more cities, and increase your army.
options.note: The opening commands concern worship and reverence, not military or urban expansion.

options.text: No commands are given at the start of this chapter.
options.note: Verses 1-2 explicitly give these commands.

options.text: Offer more sacrifices than the law requires.
options.note: Verses 1-2 concern idolatry and Sabbath keeping, not sacrificial quantity.

quiz.q: What does God promise in verses 11-12 as the culmination of the blessings for obedience?

options.text: He will make His dwelling among them, walk among them, and be their God.
options.note: Verses 11-12 state this as the pinnacle of the blessings.

options.text: Israel will never again face any hardship of any kind.
options.note: The blessings describe abundance and peace, but the specific climax named is God's own dwelling presence, not the total absence of all future hardship.

options.text: Israel would be given dominion over the entire earth immediately.
options.note: The blessings describe security in the land and victory over enemies, not immediate global dominion.

options.text: No specific blessing is described in this section.
options.note: Verses 3-13 describe blessings in significant detail.

quiz.q: What word describes Israel's posture that provokes God's escalating discipline throughout this chapter?

options.text: Walking in hostility toward Him, a settled, ongoing defiance.
options.note: This phrase, translating the rare Hebrew word qeri, recurs seven times through the curse section.

options.text: A single moment of forgetfulness.
options.note: The chapter describes ongoing, settled defiance, not a single lapse, as what provokes escalation.

options.text: Excessive generosity toward the poor.
options.note: The chapter's concern is disobedience to God's statutes, not generosity, which it commends elsewhere.

options.text: No particular posture is named.
options.note: The phrase walking in hostility recurs repeatedly and explicitly.

quiz.q: What ultimately moves God to remember His covenant, according to verses 40-42?

options.text: Israel's confession of their iniquity and the humbling of their uncircumcised hearts.
options.note: Verses 40-42 state this progression directly.

options.text: Israel achieving perfect obedience to every commandment.
options.note: The text describes confession and humbled hearts after failure, not achieved perfection, as the turning point.

options.text: Nothing; God abandons the covenant permanently at this point.
options.note: Verses 42-45 explicitly promise God will remember His covenant, not abandon it.

options.text: No condition at all is described for restoration.
options.note: Verses 40-42 explicitly describe the condition of confession and humbled hearts.

quiz.q: How does Galatians 3:13-14 relate to the curses described in this chapter?

options.text: It says Christ redeemed us from the law's curse by becoming a curse for us, so that Abraham's blessing might reach the nations.
options.note: Galatians 3:13-14 states this directly.

options.text: It denies that the law's curses were ever real or serious.
options.note: Galatians 3:13 takes the curse seriously enough that Christ must bear it Himself, rather than dismissing it.

options.text: It has no connection to the covenant curses described in Leviticus or Deuteronomy.
options.note: Galatians 3:13 speaks directly to the very curse this chapter and Deuteronomy 28 describe.

options.text: It teaches that believers must still bear the covenant curse themselves.
options.note: Galatians 3:13 teaches the opposite: Christ bore the curse in believers' place.

questions: God laid out blessings and curses in advance so Israel could never claim surprise. Where has God been clear with you about the consequences of a choice you are currently making or avoiding?

The Hebrew word for Israel's defiance describes a settled, ongoing hostility, not a single failure. Is there an area of stubborn, repeated resistance to God in your own life that has hardened past a single lapse into a pattern?

God's discipline escalated in stages, always aimed at bringing Israel back rather than simply punishing them. How do you tend to interpret hardship in your own life, as evidence of God's abandonment, or as a possible invitation to return to Him?

Even after describing exile and scattering in painful detail, God promises that confession and a humbled heart will move Him to remember His covenant. Is there a long unconfessed area of your life that needs the same honest confession this chapter describes?
Christ bore the ultimate covenant curse so that blessing might reach you. How does resting in that finished work change the way you view your own failures and God's discipline in your life today?

translationNotes.note: Five of you will pursue a hundred, and a hundred of you ten thousand uses a common Hebrew poetic device of escalating parallelism to emphasize overwhelming, disproportionate victory, not a literal mathematical ratio.
Walk in hostility translates the rare Hebrew term qeri, appearing only in this chapter in the entire Old Testament, describing a settled, defiant opposition rather than an occasional lapse.

Uncircumcised hearts uses the same physical image of circumcision metaphorically, describing stubborn resistance to God; the same image recurs in Deuteronomy 30:6 as a heart God Himself will circumcise, fulfilled in the New Testament's language of circumcision of the heart by the Spirit.
